anchor_quote: Respondent described the Service as “the world’s first robot lawyer” and an “AI lawyer” capable of performing legal services such as drafting “ironclad” demand letters, contracts, complaints for small claims court, challenging speeding tickets, and appealing parking tickets.
english_paraphrase: The complaint states that DoNotPay marketed the service as the world's first robot lawyer and an AI lawyer capable of performing multiple legal-service tasks.
claim_use: Supports narrow complaint-stage wording on the advertised legal-service-automation claims at issue.
caution_note: Complaint posture only; do not convert this into a judicial unauthorized-practice finding or proof that the service in fact performed all advertised tasks as claimed.

anchor_quote: A. that such Covered Product or Service operates like a human lawyer, including by: 1. applying the relevant laws to subscribers’ particular legal and factual situations; ... A. Respondent must pay to the Commission one-hundred-ninety-three-thousand dollars ($193,000.00) ...
english_paraphrase: The final order bars DoNotPay from representing that a covered product operates like a human lawyer in specified ways and requires payment of $193,000 to the FTC.
claim_use: Supports regulator-final wording on the prohibition scope and exact monetary-relief amount for sidecar reuse.
caution_note: Final-order posture only; do not overread settlement language as a general verdict on legal-services automation or as hidden-human final-authority closure.

anchor_quote: Respondent claims that its AI Content Detector will predict with 98.3% accuracy whether text was generated using AI technology such as ChatGPT, GPT4, Claude, Bard, or another generative AI technology. ... [Respondent] has not tested whether the AI Content Detector would achieve the same accuracy rate for marketing and other plain language text that Respondent’s users typically submit.
english_paraphrase: The complaint states that Workado claimed 98.3% detector accuracy across major generative-AI systems while lacking testing on the plain-language text its users typically submitted.
claim_use: Supports narrow complaint-stage wording on the advertised detector-accuracy claim and the alleged testing mismatch for shared authoritative-core detector-governance reuse.
caution_note: Complaint posture only; do not convert this into a general scientific verdict about all AI detectors or a settled finding about detector performance outside the FTC record.

anchor_quote: Respondent ... must not make any representation ... about such product’s effectiveness at detecting content generated or altered by artificial intelligence, unless the representation is non-misleading ... [and supported by] competent and reliable evidence ... The order would also require that Workado retain all evidence used to substantiate a covered claim, and it would require providing notice about this settlement to customers who purchased a subscription to the AI Detector.
english_paraphrase: The final order bars misleading or unsubstantiated detector-effectiveness representations and adds evidence-retention and customer-notice obligations.
claim_use: Supports bounded order-stage wording on substantiation, recordkeeping, and customer-notice duties for shared authoritative-core detector-governance reuse.
caution_note: Final-order posture only; do not restate the order as a general detector-validity ruling or a broad educational-process finding.
